canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, SEED-Bench-2 is consistently characterized as a multimodal benchmark for evaluating language and vision models. Its most frequently mentioned application is assessing a model's ability to handle multiple images, with one paper defining its purpose as evaluating the "ability to comprehend and reason about multimodal inputs containing multiple images" (Smurfs: Multi-Agent System using Context-EfficientDFSDTfor Tool Planning). This benchmark is explicitly used to measure the construct of "Understanding" in this multi-image context. This focus is extended by another study that frames it as a benchmark for "long-context tasks that require identifying relevant information from image sequences" (MatViX: Multimodal Information Extraction from Visually Rich Articles), a use supported by its relationship with "Long-context processing". A more general application is also described, where the benchmark is used to evaluate the "general performance of Vision-Language Assistants," particularly after debiasing methods have been applied. In practice, it is used alongside other multimodal benchmarks and also serves as a "non-synthetic comparison point" to validate the evaluation rankings produced by other systems.
papers: Dysca: A Dynamic and Scalable Benchmark for Evaluating Perception Ability of LVLMs
Revealing and Reducing Gender Biases in Vision and Language Assistants (VLAs)
MatViX: Multimodal Information Extraction from Visually Rich Articles
Smurfs: Multi-Agent System using Context-EfficientDFSDTfor Tool Planning

definitions.text: Seed-Bench-2 (Li et al., 2023a; 2024a) is a multimodal benchmark used to evaluate the general performance of Vision-Language Assistants after applying debiasing methods.

definitions.text: SeedBench-2, a benchmark used in the paper as a non-synthetic comparison point for LVLM evaluation rankings.

Its most frequently mentioned application is assessing a model's ability to handle multiple images, with one paper defining its purpose as evaluating the "ability to comprehend and reason about multimodal inputs containing multiple images" (Smurfs: Multi-Agent System using Context-EfficientDFSDTfor Tool Planning). This benchmark is explicitly used to measure the construct of "Understanding" in this multi-image context. This focus is extended by another study that frames it as a benchmark for "long-context tasks that require identifying relevant information from image sequences" (MatViX: Multimodal Information Extraction from Visually Rich Articles), a use supported by its relationship with "Long-context processing". A more general application is also described, where the benchmark is used to evaluate the "general performance of Vision-Language Assistants," particularly after debiasing methods have been applied. In practice, it is used alongside other multimodal benchmarks and also serves as a "non-synthetic comparison point" to validate the evaluation rankings produced by other systems.
